title: How to Set Up and Use SpaceBot (Better Than OpenClaw?)
summary: SpaceBot is an open-source framework designed for building AI agents integrated with community platforms like Discord, Telegram, Slack, Twitch, and WebChat. The tutorial demonstrates Docker installation, model routing for token efficiency, and multi-agent capabilities with features including memory, cron jobs, and platform bindings.
writeup: SpaceBot is a community agent framework (under 2,000 GitHub stars) built for Discord, Slack, Telegram, Twitch, and WebChat, and this tutorial installs it into a Telegram group via Docker, wires it to the Venice LLM provider, and configures a meme-scraping persona. The through-line is that SpaceBot ships working, opinionated defaults: a typed-graph knowledge system instead of flat markdown or unstructured vector blocks, four-level model routing to match cost to task complexity, and natural-language scheduling baked in. It is the rare community agent that actually behaves as advertised, convincing users it is human without heavy prompt engineering.
key_points: SpaceBot stores agent memory as a typed graph connected knowledge system rather than markdown files or unstructured vector database blocks, and the tutorial credits this architecture for the framework's reliable out-of-the-box behavior.

Built-in model routing supports four levels, allowing cheap models (Haiku, GLM Flash, Ollama) to handle low-stakes tasks like git commits while reserving stronger models (Sonnet, Opus) for higher-stakes work such as writing PRDs, keeping token costs proportional to task complexity.

Native platform adapters cover Discord, Slack, Telegram, Twitch, and WebChat; natural-language scheduling works without configuration (example: setting a daily 8 a.m. check-in message required only a plain English instruction to the bot).

The install path demonstrated uses OpenCode (with GLM-5, an open-source model) to pull the Docker image and scaffold the config files, then populates provider credentials for Venice (an OpenAI-compatible endpoint), a Telegram bot token, and a plain-text identity file that defines persona, speech patterns, and skills.

A live deployment named Captain Jack in a Discord server went undetected as an AI by at least one community member, and the bot successfully responded to a direct user challenge (posting two consecutive messages) without admin intervention, illustrating personality persistence under low prompt engineering.

SpaceBot also supports MCP integration and can spawn an embedded Claude Code coding agent, which enabled a collaborative session where the community and the bot co-designed a learning platform PRD that was later vibe-coded as an MVP prototype.
